chapters.title: A future president with no political family tree
chapters.text:
No one in Obama's family imagined he would end up in the White House. Politics was not part of his upbringing, and he had little interest in it during his early years. Instead, he focused on the people close to him and the causes that mattered most to him.

He describes his mother as a woman who refused to follow rules, yet showed warmth and love in everything she did. She often spoke about civil rights and the protests that were unfolding across the country. After his birth in Hawaii, the family moved to Indonesia for a time, and his mother grew frustrated with the corruption she saw there.

Obama says his sense of justice came directly from her. She always stood up for people who had less and called out anyone who abused their power.

His hunger for change also traces back to his mother. She saw the world as full of possibility, a view she passed on to him. Yet she never cared about politics, and neither did he for a long time. His grandparents in Hawaii, where he grew up with little money, believed he would amount to something, but even they never pictured him as President.

As a teenager, he returned to Hawaii to live with his grandparents while his mother stayed in Indonesia for work. He credits a book sale around that time with shifting his outlook. Reading everything he could find opened his eyes to new ideas and sparked his first real interest in politics and public life.

Obama has said that what drew him in was not campaigns but movements, where ordinary people came together to push for change.

At Columbia University, Obama studied hard. He focused on what America meant to him, a place where every person counted and no one was set apart. He went on to Harvard Law School, where he finished his education.

He kept the view that America should be a country where no one is treated as less than anyone else.

chapters.title: Two people who fit together
chapters.text:
Michelle Obama was just twenty five when she first met the man she would marry. She was Michelle Robinson then, a law student working at a firm in Chicago. Obama came to the same firm for a summer job during his first year at Harvard Law. Michelle was asked to look after him and help him get settled. They became close friends at first, and that friendship grew into something deeper.

Michelle has always been a steady, firm woman. She does not bend easily during a disagreement. Obama says that is what first caught his eye. Whenever he shared a new plan, and he had plenty, she did not flinch. She simply encouraged him and said he should do whatever he needed. His first plan was to move back to Chicago and work in both law and community organizing, with a focus on civil rights.

Her steady support has been central to his success. She has stood beside him through ideas that many thought would never work.

In 1992, the two married. At that point, Obama still had no interest in elected office. He wanted to keep helping people through civil rights work. In 1997, Michelle gave birth to their first daughter, Sasha, and later they had Malia. Obama thought about slowing down and spending more time at home, maybe writing, but his ambitions would not fade. As the Democratic Party started winning again, his interest in politics grew. He saw a chance to make a real difference. That was when he decided to run for the United States Senate.

Obama has written that a new senator often struggles just to be heard, but he found the opposite problem. People could not stop paying attention.

chapters.title: Pursuing the impossible
chapters.text:
Obama entered politics without ever aiming for the presidency. He never believed it would actually happen. Many people today pat him on the back and claim they always knew he would reach the top, but he was far from sure himself. The idea did not become real until early 2006. His Senate office was flooded with media requests, and everyone wanted to know more about this young, charismatic newcomer.

After studying the polls and weighing every option, his team concluded that Obama was one of the strongest Democratic candidates for president. Obama said he would only agree to run if he could use the campaign to change how people saw the country and help minorities feel as valued as everyone else.

He built his campaign around equality and the belief that every person matters. He wanted to make America better, and that goal sat at the heart of his message.

He launched his bid for the Democratic nomination in Springfield, the same spot where Lincoln had kicked off his own campaign. He did not expect a crowd, yet close to fifteen thousand people showed up. In that first speech, he laid out his main goals: reform healthcare, address climate change, and bring people together. He ran against Hillary Clinton and admits he made mistakes along the way, but he kept working hard.

He used his platform and the color of his skin to drive home a simple message: we are all the same, we are all equal. He ran into plenty of barriers. At the end of the primary, he won the Democratic nomination for the 2008 presidential race and faced John McCain in the general election. He quickly realized that winning the required number of votes was not just about his policy ideas. It was also about whether Americans could accept a young, inexperienced, Black president.

His battle against McCain came down to whether the country was ready to take a chance on a new direction. Lacking experience, Obama made up for it with energy and passion.

He picked Joe Biden, a seasoned politician, as his running mate. They were opposites in character and background, but Obama felt Biden brought a balance no one else could. He also saw kindness and a real hunger for change in Biden. Having lost his first wife and daughter in a car crash and later his son Beau, Biden knew grief and empathy as well as anyone.

When the votes were counted, Obama won comfortably over McCain and became the forty fourth President of the United States.

chapters.title: Early days tested by fire
chapters.text:
Obama's first visit to the Oval Office came when the outgoing President, George Bush, invited him for a tour, as is the custom for the President Elect. The room is smaller than most people picture, but it fills with light and carries a quiet weight. Obama spent long hours there during his two terms and never took its traditions for granted. He came to see the Oval Office as a place where democracy stands firm.

He treated the Oval Office as a symbol of democracy, a daily reminder of his duty to the American people.

His first task as President was to deal with a collapsing economy. Unemployment was sky high, and the markets and banks were on the verge of total failure. Speed mattered, and his team moved quickly. At the same time, Obama was building his administration and wanted to heal the wounds from the primary fight. To that end, he chose Hillary Clinton as his Secretary of State.

In his first month in office, he pushed through the Recovery Act to steady the economy. The recovery did not come right away, but the speed of action showed he did not plan to move slowly. The American Recovery and Reinvestment Act followed close behind.

Within his first month, Obama signed two major laws aimed at rebuilding a struggling economy.

During this period, Michelle and the girls settled into the White House. The children baked cookies with the kitchen staff and ran through the halls. Michelle focused her energy on a campaign against childhood obesity and on supporting military families.

With his family living where he worked, Obama realized he needed a healthy balance between home and office, making real time for his wife and daughters.

chapters.title: Learning the rhythm of global summits
chapters.text:
Over his two terms, Obama got used to the boredom and long speeches that came with international summits. For all their importance, the events were often packed with routines that wasted time and cut into real work.

He soon discovered that summits were not glamorous at all. He used them as chances to hold quiet side conversations with other leaders.

Obama has compared these summits to the flashbulbs at a Hollywood premiere: bright on the outside, dull once you step inside. As he gained experience, he started carrying extra paperwork to fill quiet stretches, and he got good at catching a leader for a private word when someone else was speaking too long.

At his first summit in London, he chose to play the newcomer. He listened carefully, stayed focused, and knew all eyes were on him. He figured a little humility might help him build goodwill with other leaders. By showing that respect, he hoped to push economic plans through with less resistance.

Watching foreign leaders closely at that first summit helped Obama figure out the right approach for each of them.

Across many meetings with foreign leaders, Obama carefully managed expectations. He sized up leaders at his first summit, then used what he learned in later discussions.

chapters.title: A showdown with Somali pirates
chapters.text:
Right after his first G20 summit in London, Obama faced his first national security crisis and terrorist incident. There would be many more over his two terms, but the first one stayed with him.

As he boarded Air Force One to head back to Washington, he was told that a cargo ship with an American captain had been seized by Somali pirates. Piracy was a long-standing problem off the African coast, but the fact that an American was captain raised the stakes. For four days, Obama and his national security team tracked the situation and worked on a plan. Thanks to quick action by the crew, the pirates left the ship after a few days, thinking it was no longer seaworthy. But as the American captain tried to escape, the pirates grabbed him.

Obama faced his first security crisis with Somali pirates. With no real option but to shoot the pirates and save the captain, Obama took no pride in the deaths of the Somalis.

He had to act fast. He ordered Navy SEAL snipers to fire on the pirates if the captain was in danger. When the pirates stepped into the open with a gun to the captain head, the snipers took the shot. The captain was saved and the pirates were killed on impact. The media called it a win for Obama in his first major crisis, but he felt nothing like celebrating.

Obama always tried to see the other side. These pirates were young, and desperation and manipulation by those around them had pushed them into this. The same pattern played out across the world, especially in the Middle East, where young men were often radicalized with wild promises of religious glory. Obama has said plainly that the job had to be done, but it was a part of the work he never enjoyed.

Around the world, desperation and radicalization drive young people to desperate acts. Obama kept that in mind as he dealt with these moments.

chapters.title: The Affordable Care Act takes hold
chapters.text:
One of the defining achievements of Obama presidency was the Affordable Care Act, also known as Obamacare. Built to make health coverage affordable for everyone, including people with pre existing conditions, the law passed in 2010.

Attempts to fix American healthcare were nothing new and went all the way back to Theodore Roosevelt. Costs had been rising for years, and when Obama took office, about forty three million Americans had no health insurance at all.

The Affordable Care Act, also called Obamacare, passed in 2010 and helped make healthcare more available to everyone.

Obama saw healthcare reform as deeply personal, far more than a political issue. Watching his own family grow older and losing his mother pushed him to act. He had seen the struggles they faced and vowed to do something about it for the millions of Americans in the same situation.

Healthcare had been a central promise of his campaign, along with rebuilding the economy, tackling climate change, and making America safer. That record helped him win the Nobel Peace Prize. He was surprised at first to be chosen, and he used his speech in Oslo to honor other world leaders who were working for positive change.

When he accepted the Nobel Peace Prize, Obama looked past the award itself and used it as a reason to keep fighting for lasting change.

chapters.title: Trump wild conspiracy theories
chapters.text:
Donald Trump began to cause real problems for Obama during the second half of his presidency. That was when Trump started pushing the false claim that Obama had not been born in America and therefore could not legally be President.

Trump spread the untrue claim that Obama was born in Kenya, not the United States. The rumors ended when Obama released his birth certificate, rising above the noise Trump was trying to create.

At first, Obama paid no attention. He had been born in Hawaii and had the paper to prove it, having shown it once before when the same story surfaced. But this time Trump pushed the idea harder, and the claims kept getting more public airtime. He seemed to be on Fox News or another network almost every day, talking about what he claimed Obama was hiding. Trump insisted Obama had been born in Kenya, and new, ever more outlandish claims piled onto the original one.

In Obama telling, Trump offered a simple answer to millions of Americans who were uneasy about a Black man in the White House.

After ignoring the noise for a while, Obama realized that Trump was mainly after attention, but the stunts were starting to cause real damage. Obama asked for his long form birth certificate to be released, proving he was born in Hawaii. After a press conference meant to close the matter once and for all, he told his staff they were all better than this and he was glad it was over.

chapters.title: The end of Bin Laden
chapters.text:
Of all the military choices Obama made during his two terms, one stands above the rest: the raid on the compound believed to be hiding Osama Bin Laden, the most wanted man in America.

Bin Laden had managed to stay out of reach for years and had come close to being caught only once since 9/11. Obama was told the CIA believed Bin Laden was living in a compound in Pakistan. They could not be certain, but they put the odds at sixty forty. After tracking the lead for several weeks, Obama faced a choice: go in and try to capture or kill the man behind so much American grief, or get it badly wrong.

Bin Laden was the mastermind of the 9/11 attacks. Catching him sat near the top of Obama list of priorities in office.

The planning was done without telling the Pakistani government. Even though they had helped with other counterterrorism efforts, the team did not want any chance of a leak that would let Bin Laden slip away. After several plans were drawn up, the call came to Obama, and he gave the go ahead.

Obama and his team, including Hillary Clinton, watched the Navy SEALs carry out the raid in real time. They had prepared for both outcomes: a burial at sea in line with Islamic tradition if the target was killed, or bringing him back to the United States if he survived. They watched the action unfold in the Situation Room of the White House. As the Black Hawk helicopters closed in on the compound, Obama felt tense, and time seemed to slow.

After what felt like an eternity, the radio crackled. The man inside the compound was indeed Bin Laden, and he had been killed in the raid. Obama felt a wave of relief for the families who had lost loved ones at Bin Laden hands. The media around the world praised the operation. It is probably the single most memorable moment of his two terms.

Osama Bin Laden was killed on May 2, 2011. Obama was widely praised for ordering the raid and for giving the families of 9/11 victims a measure of peace.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
Barack Obama is a former President who remains popular with many Americans. After serving the maximum two terms over eight years, he still plays a role in public life and was recently seen speaking in support of Joe Biden, his former Vice President, during the transition to a new administration.

Across his two terms, Obama pushed for positive change and worked hard to bring people together in a world that often feels divided. He faced many obstacles and admits he made a few mistakes along the way, but he tried to lead with empathy and kindness, always looking for ways to help as many people as possible. Where some politicians chase personal agendas, Obama lack of a political background gave him a different kind of edge. He preferred to focus on change that would make life better for future generations. That is the legacy he will leave behind, and it is why so many people look back on his time in office with respect.

A few things you can try.

Obama often looked for ways to help others during his early years at Harvard Law School. You can make a small positive change today. Look for a simple good deed to do each day and notice the difference it makes.

Even while holding the most high profile job in the world, Obama always made time for his family. Take a look at your own balance between home and work. Are there changes you need to make?

You may not be able to change the nation, but you can make a difference close to home. Are there local causes you can support, or people nearby who could use a hand?

If you read all the way to the end, well done.
